audit_metadata.methodology: Sampled 5 random questions per concept (seed=42), evaluated whether question content genuinely matches the taxonomy_concept label

rating_criteria.HIGH: >80% of questions correctly match the concept
rating_criteria.MEDIUM: 50-80% of questions correctly match the concept
rating_criteria.LOW: <50% of questions correctly match the concept

systemic_issues: CATCH-ALL BUCKET PROBLEM: Many taxonomy_concept values appear to be the 'default' concept for an entire topic area rather than a specific concept. E.g., 'block-wise-quantization' captures ALL quantization questions, 'pcie' captures ALL interconnect questions, 'blue-green-deployment' captures ALL deployment questions.

NAMING MISMATCH: Concept names suggest specific sub-topics (block-wise quantization, blue-green deployment, chiplet architecture) but are applied as broad category labels. This makes the taxonomy unreliable for filtering or studying specific concepts.
GPU-CENTRIC NAMING: 'gpu-memory-hierarchy' is applied to MCU/edge memory questions. The naming assumes GPU context but the content spans all hardware tiers.
COMPOSITE CONCEPTS: 'energy-roofline-model' conflates energy analysis with roofline model analysis — two distinct concepts bundled under one label.

WORST OFFENDERS (0/5 correct): blue-green-deployment (120 Qs), chiplet-architecture (42 Qs), activation-logic-unit-cost (84 Qs) — these three concepts are essentially random labels with no correlation to question content.
ESTIMATED CORPUS IMPACT: Of the top 20 concepts (covering ~2,582 questions, 49.7% of corpus), approximately 40% are misclassified. This suggests ~1,000+ questions have wrong taxonomy_concept values.

concept_audits.analysis: The concept 'energy-roofline-model' conflates two distinct ideas: roofline model analysis and energy/power analysis. Questions about roofline (compute vs memory bound, arithmetic intensity) are correctly tagged. However, questions purely about datacenter power budgets (TDP, rack power) have no roofline component and are misclassified.

misclassified_examples.title: The Datacenter Power Wall
misclassified_examples.reason: Question is purely about GPU TDP and server rack power budgets. No roofline model, arithmetic intensity, or compute/memory bound analysis involved.

misclassified_examples.title: The Tensor Core Utilization Gap
misclassified_examples.should_be: roofline-model (correct roofline, but 'energy' prefix is misleading)
misclassified_examples.reason: This is a valid roofline question about tensor core utilization and hidden_dim sizing, but the 'energy' prefix in the concept name is misleading — the question has no energy component.

concept_audits.analysis: Severe misclassification. 'Block-wise quantization' is a specific technique (quantizing weights in blocks with per-block scale factors, e.g., GPTQ, AWQ). Most questions tagged with this concept are about general quantization topics: INT8 energy savings, zero-point calibration, overflow/saturation, FP16-to-INT8 conversion. These are quantization questions, but NOT about block-wise quantization specifically.

misclassified_examples.title: The INT8 Energy Dividend
misclassified_examples.reason: Question is about FP32-to-INT8 energy reduction for MAC operations. No block-wise granularity, scale factors, or block structure mentioned.

misclassified_examples.title: The FP16 vs. INT8 Energy Tax
misclassified_examples.reason: Compares energy cost of FP16 vs INT8 MAC operations. This is precision-level energy analysis, not block-wise quantization.

misclassified_examples.title: The Zero-Point Question
misclassified_examples.reason: About zero-point offset in uniform quantization. This is a fundamental quantization concept, not block-wise quantization.

misclassified_examples.title: The Saturation Misfire
misclassified_examples.reason: About INT8 calibration range overflow causing saturation in deployment. No block-wise structure involved.

concept_audits.analysis: All sampled questions are genuinely about federated learning: TCO of FL vs centralized, bandwidth costs of gradient uploads, privacy tradeoffs, fleet data egress. This concept is correctly applied.

The concept name says 'gpu-memory-hierarchy' but many questions are about MCU/edge memory hierarchies (SRAM, ITCM, flash, tensor arenas on Cortex-M4). These are memory hierarchy questions, but NOT GPU memory hierarchy. The concept name is too narrow for the content it covers.

misclassified_examples.title: The SRAM Memory Ceiling
misclassified_examples.reason: About Cortex-M4 SRAM constraints. No GPU involved — this is microcontroller memory hierarchy.

misclassified_examples.title: The ITCM Execution Bottleneck
misclassified_examples.reason: About ITCM/QSPI Flash execution on an NXP MCU. Entirely MCU-specific, no GPU involvement.

misclassified_examples.title: The Tensor Arena Overflow
misclassified_examples.reason: About TFLite Micro tensor arena fitting into Cortex-M4 SRAM. MCU memory management, not GPU.

concept_audits.analysis: 'Application-embedded serving' refers to serving ML models embedded within applications (on-device, in-app inference). Some questions match (on-device copilot, MCU gateway), but cloud LLM serving questions about KV-cache swapping, P99 latency, and multi-modal token starvation are cloud serving infrastructure — not application-embedded.

misclassified_examples.title: The KV-Cache Swapping Cliff
misclassified_examples.reason: About vLLM KV-cache CPU swapping on cloud GPU infrastructure. This is cloud serving optimization, not application-embedded serving.

misclassified_examples.title: The P99 Latency Anomaly
misclassified_examples.reason: About P99 TTFT anomalies with static batching on H100s. Cloud LLM serving, not embedded in an application.

misclassified_examples.title: The Multi-Modal Token Starvation
misclassified_examples.reason: About multi-modal LLM serving with vision encoders and GPU utilization during decode. Cloud infrastructure serving, not application-embedded.

concept_audits.analysis: Severe misclassification. 'Real-time fairness monitoring' refers to monitoring ML models for fairness/bias in real-time. Only 1 of 5 sampled questions touches monitoring (federated analytics dashboard). The rest are about real-time latency constraints (batching deadlines, perception pipeline deadlines, audio packet drops, jank frames) — these are real-time systems questions, not fairness monitoring.

misclassified_examples.title: The Real-Time Batching Deadline
misclassified_examples.reason: About LLM inference batching with P99 latency SLOs on H100. No fairness or monitoring component.

misclassified_examples.title: The Perception Pipeline's Deadline
misclassified_examples.reason: About meeting 33ms hard real-time deadlines for autonomous vehicle perception. No fairness monitoring.

misclassified_examples.title: The Dropped Audio Packet
misclassified_examples.reason: About Cortex-M4 wakeword detector meeting real-time audio processing deadlines. No fairness or monitoring.

misclassified_examples.title: The Jank Frame Catastrophe
misclassified_examples.reason: About GPU contention causing jank in a mobile video filter app. Real-time scheduling, not fairness monitoring.

concept_audits.analysis: The CMOS power equation (P = alpha * C * V^2 * f + P_leak) is a specific physics equation. Only 2 of 5 questions relate to it (power-aware scheduler touching DVFS, and the datacenter PUE question tangentially references power fundamentals). The others are about duty cycling, thermal derating, and battery life calculations which don't involve the CMOS power equation.

misclassified_examples.title: The Energy-Aware Reconnaissance Drone
misclassified_examples.reason: About dynamic power budgeting for a drone mission. Scheduling inference based on power budget, not the CMOS equation.

misclassified_examples.title: Battery Life for Solar-Powered Edge Device
misclassified_examples.reason: About solar charging and battery life calculations for a wildlife camera. Energy harvesting math, not CMOS power equation.

misclassified_examples.title: The Thermal Derating Curve
misclassified_examples.reason: About thermal throttling at high ambient temperatures. TDP derating, not the CMOS switching power equation.

concept_audits.analysis: Catastrophic misclassification. Blue-green deployment is a specific deployment strategy (two identical environments, switch traffic between them). ZERO of 5 sampled questions are about blue-green deployment. They cover: bandwidth-constrained OTA updates, ephemeral storage failures, cron job stragglers, self-healing edge fleets, and model reproducibility. These are deployment/operations questions, but none involve the blue-green pattern.

misclassified_examples.title: The Bandwidth-Constrained Model Update
misclassified_examples.reason: About delta model updates over constrained cellular bandwidth. No blue-green deployment pattern.

misclassified_examples.title: The Ephemeral Storage I/O Cliff
misclassified_examples.reason: About spot instance preemption and ephemeral storage data loss. Cloud ops, not blue-green deployment.

misclassified_examples.title: The Straggler Log Rotation
misclassified_examples.reason: About a cron job causing training stalls across 512 GPUs. Systems debugging, not blue-green deployment.

misclassified_examples.title: Self-Healing Edge AI Fleet
misclassified_examples.reason: About designing self-healing systems for 5000 edge devices. Fleet management, not blue-green deployment.

misclassified_examples.title: The Unreproducible Model
misclassified_examples.reason: About model reproducibility and provenance tracking. MLOps, not blue-green deployment.

concept_audits.analysis: All sampled questions genuinely involve latency analysis: audio pipeline latency creep, inference timing jitter, LLM queueing latency, ANR timeouts, interconnect latency ordering. The concept is broad but accurately applied.
All sampled questions are directly about depthwise separable convolutions: computational reduction ratios, replacing standard convolutions, Smart Doorbell optimization. Well-classified concept.

The 'data-pipeline-equation' concept implies a specific equation governing data pipeline throughput. While 2 questions relate to data pipeline bottlenecks (ISP format conversion, YUV conversion), others are about cache-line striding in matrix multiplication, ADC jitter from power supply noise, and DMA audio sample dropouts — these are memory access patterns, signal integrity, and real-time systems issues, not data pipeline equations.

misclassified_examples.title: The Striding Stumble
misclassified_examples.reason: About row-major vs column-major matrix access patterns and cache line utilization. This is memory hierarchy/cache optimization, not a data pipeline equation.

misclassified_examples.title: The Real-Time Audio Sample Dropout
misclassified_examples.reason: About I2S DMA double-buffering to prevent audio sample drops on Cortex-M4. Real-time embedded systems, not data pipeline equation.

misclassified_examples.title: The Power Supply ADC Jitter
misclassified_examples.reason: About ADC noise from power supply ripple degrading model accuracy. Signal integrity, not data pipeline equation.

concept_audits.analysis: Catastrophic misclassification. 'Activation-logic-unit-cost' should refer to the computational cost of activation functions or ALU operations. ZERO of 5 sampled questions match: they cover shared GPU contention on mobile, heterogeneous MCU/NPU task assignment, video encoding bottlenecks, GQA/MQA KV-cache memory, and NCCL NVLink deadlocks. These span completely unrelated topics.

misclassified_examples.title: The Shared GPU Contention
misclassified_examples.reason: About GPU contention between ML inference and UI rendering on mobile. Not about activation function or ALU costs.

misclassified_examples.title: Cortex-M55 + Ethos-U55 + Cortex-A32 — Which Core Runs What?
misclassified_examples.reason: About workload partitioning across heterogeneous MCU/NPU cores. No ALU cost analysis.

misclassified_examples.title: The Ambarella CV5 Encoding Bottleneck
misclassified_examples.reason: About shared memory bandwidth between video encoder and neural network accelerator. Not ALU cost.

misclassified_examples.title: The GQA/MQA Memory Bottleneck
misclassified_examples.reason: About GQA reducing KV-cache size and memory bandwidth implications. Attention mechanism, not ALU cost.

misclassified_examples.title: The NCCL NVLink Deadlock
misclassified_examples.reason: About NCCL ring topology failing with non-power-of-2 GPU counts. Interconnect topology, not ALU cost.

concept_audits.analysis: 3D parallelism (data + tensor + pipeline parallelism) is a specific distributed training strategy. Questions about DDP, tensor parallelism (Megatron-LM), and context parallelism are related. However, questions about edge AV training on 2 accelerators and basic heterogeneous GPU DDP don't involve 3D parallelism — they are simpler data parallelism scenarios.

misclassified_examples.title: The AV Training Bottleneck
misclassified_examples.reason: Two accelerators doing data parallel training on an autonomous vehicle. Simple 2-device DP, not 3D parallelism.

misclassified_examples.title: The Heterogeneous GPU Training
misclassified_examples.reason: About mixing A100 and H100 GPUs in DDP. This is data parallelism with heterogeneous hardware, not 3D parallelism.

concept_audits.analysis: All sampled questions genuinely address fault tolerance: watchdog timers, checkpoint corruption, split-brain scenarios, resilient automotive ML systems. Well-classified.

Most questions are genuinely about TCO: infrastructure costs, energy costs, maintenance taxes, cost component analysis. One edge case (OTA update cost) is more about deployment logistics than TCO analysis, though it has a cost component.

misclassified_examples.title: The OTA Update Budget Shock
misclassified_examples.should_be: ota-model-update (borderline — has cost component but is primarily about deployment logistics)
misclassified_examples.reason: While it involves cost calculation, the core question is about OTA update bandwidth and delivery strategy, not comprehensive TCO analysis.

concept_audits.analysis: Most questions correctly involve duty cycle calculations: wildlife sensor average power, battery drain with duty cycling. One misclassified question about VRAM/memory footprint calculation (model weight size in FP16) has no duty cycle component.

misclassified_examples.title: The App Budget Constraint
misclassified_examples.reason: About calculating FP16 model weight memory footprint (750M params * 2 bytes). No duty cycle or power cycling involved.

concept_audits.analysis: PCIe is a specific interconnect standard. Only 1 of 5 questions is about PCIe or closely related bus transfers. The rest cover: DDP gradient synchronization overlap (AllReduce), InfiniBand vs RoCE decisions, disconnected edge robots, and LoRaWAN OTA congestion. These are interconnect/networking questions broadly, but NOT about PCIe specifically.

misclassified_examples.title: The Gradient Synchronization Overlap
misclassified_examples.reason: About DDP backward pass / AllReduce overlap. Communication optimization, not PCIe-specific.

misclassified_examples.title: The InfiniBand vs RoCE Decision
misclassified_examples.reason: Comparing InfiniBand NDR vs RoCE v2 for a training cluster. Network fabric decision, not PCIe.

misclassified_examples.title: The Disconnected Brain
misclassified_examples.reason: About agricultural robots with intermittent cellular connectivity. Wireless networking, not PCIe.

misclassified_examples.title: The OTA Bandwidth Congestion
misclassified_examples.reason: About LoRaWAN bandwidth congestion during OTA updates. IoT wireless networking, not PCIe.

concept_audits.analysis: Most questions are genuinely about continuous batching: PagedAttention, static-to-continuous migration, batching tradeoffs, tail latency. One mobile question about real-time translation is more about on-device scheduling than continuous batching.

misclassified_examples.title: The Real-Time Translation Deadline
misclassified_examples.reason: About meeting 16ms render deadlines for on-device translation. The batching here is basic request scheduling, not the continuous batching (PagedAttention) paradigm.

concept_audits.analysis: Catastrophic misclassification. Chiplet architecture refers to chip packaging where multiple smaller dies (chiplets) are interconnected on a single package (e.g., AMD EPYC, Intel Ponte Vecchio). ZERO of 5 sampled questions are about chiplet architecture. They cover: CNN-to-ViT migration for AVs, mobile ViT jank debugging, NAS/MoE for autonomous trucks, TOPS/W efficiency metrics, and self-attention latency traps.

misclassified_examples.title: The Autonomous Fleet's Perception Upgrade
misclassified_examples.reason: About CNN vs ViT tradeoffs for autonomous vehicle perception. No chiplet architecture.

misclassified_examples.title: The Mobile Jank Detective
misclassified_examples.reason: About profiling ViT inference jank on mobile NPU using roofline analysis. No chiplet architecture.

misclassified_examples.title: The Autonomous Truck Redesign
misclassified_examples.reason: About NAS and MoE for autonomous truck perception. Model architecture, not chip packaging.

misclassified_examples.title: The Efficiency Litmus Test
misclassified_examples.reason: About TOPS/W as efficiency metric for comparing edge accelerators. Not about chiplet packaging.

misclassified_examples.title: The Self-Attention Latency Trap
misclassified_examples.reason: About quadratic self-attention causing latency issues on edge. No chiplet architecture.

concept_audits.analysis: All sampled questions relate to thermal design power: thermal throttling on mobile, robot dog overheating, rack power density limits, warm-up performance drops, high-altitude thermal failures. Well-classified.
